title: Arrays and Two Pointers
learningObjectives: Explain Arrays and Two Pointers with interview-grade clarity
Implement Arrays and Two Pointers solutions in clean Java
Choose the right pattern and justify complexity
Handle boundary cases without brittle logic
Use this topic to solve coding-round problems confidently

sections.title: Why Arrays and Two Pointers matters
sections.content:
Two-pointer patterns turn many O(n^2) brute-force array problems into O(n) or O(n log n) solutions.

This day is central to DSA interviews because it combines coding speed with reasoning quality. Interviewers usually evaluate three things:
1. Can you identify the right pattern quickly?
2. Can you explain why the approach is correct?
3. Can you reason about complexity and edge cases?

Sliding windows, pointer movement logic, and invariant maintenance.

A high-quality answer should include:
- concise approach summary,
- small dry-run,
- complexity analysis,
- one alternative approach and trade-off.

By the end of Day 20, you should be able to solve representative problems and explain your decisions with confidence.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Arrays and Two Pointers
sections.content:
### Why this topic matters now
Two-pointer patterns turn many O(n^2) brute-force array problems into O(n) or O(n log n) solutions.

### Core interview mental model
For Arrays and Two Pointers, first identify the problem family, then map it to a known pattern. Pattern-first thinking reduces random trial-and-error.

### Implementation guidance
1. Write the simplest correct version first.
2. Validate using one normal and one edge case.
3. Improve complexity only after correctness is locked.
4. Refactor to readable helper methods.

### Complexity and trade-off lens
Do not state complexity by memory. Derive it from operations performed. Mention time + space and where each cost comes from.

### Common failure mode
Most failures in Arrays and Two Pointers are not syntax errors. They come from broken invariants, weak boundary checks, or unclear state tracking.

### Interview speaking template
- "I considered X because..."
- "The invariant is..."
- "Per iteration we do..., so complexity is..."
- "An alternative would be..., but I chose this due to..."

### Production relevance
Even outside interviews, these patterns improve throughput, latency, and correctness in backend data processing tasks.

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**Two pointers** often turn O(n²) into O(n) on sorted arrays — pair sum, triplet, container with most water pattern.

**Sliding window** for contiguous subarray problems — expand/shrink while maintaining invariant.

**Prefix sums** answer range queries in O(1) after O(n) prep — essential for competitive and analytics code.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Two pointers from both ends: palindrome check on char array.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Sorted array + two pointers: find pair summing to target in O(n).

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Container with most water style: move the pointer at the shorter line.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Moving both pointers incorrectly and skipping valid candidates.
Forgetting to update window state when left pointer shifts.
Not defining the window invariant before coding.
Applying two pointers on unsorted data without justification.
Missing edge cases for empty/single-element arrays.

sections.title: Exercise - Arrays and Two Pointers Problem Drill
sections.problem:
Solve one medium-level Java problem for **Arrays and Two Pointers**.

Requirements:
1. Provide brute-force and optimized approach.
2. Explain why optimized approach is better.
3. Include one edge-case test.
4. Print expected output for sample input.
5. State time/space complexity clearly.
hints: Dry-run on paper for one non-trivial case.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is the two-pointer technique?
conceptual.answer: Two indices move through array/string—often from both ends or both forward—to reduce brute force O(n²) to O(n).

conceptual.answer: Sorted arrays, palindromes, pair-sum with ordering, merging sorted lists—monotonic movement exploits structure.

wrongAnswers: Two pointers always require sorted input.
Two-pointer pair sum is O(n²) after sorting.
Fast/slow pointers only work on arrays, never linked lists.
You never need hash map if you use two pointers.
Three-sum is always O(n log n).
Two pointers from both ends always move both every step.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Day 20 Focus | Quick Reminder | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Arrays and Two Pointers | Choose pattern before coding | Random brute-force wandering |
| Correctness | Invariant + edge checks | Dry-run once | Off-by-one / null-state miss |
| Complexity | Derive, do not guess | Mention time + space | Incomplete complexity answers |
| Communication | Explain decisions | Use trade-off statement | Generic textbook responses |
| Quality | Readable Java methods | Stable naming + small units | Clever but fragile logic |
